items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What is the difference between glucose and sucrose?
items.correct: Glucose is a monosaccharide (single sugar); sucrose is a disaccharide (glucose + fructose).
items.random_wrong: Glucose is artificial, sucrose is natural.
items.why: Students treat all sugars as identical. Sucrose (table sugar) is two sugars bonded together - the body splits it into glucose and fructose during digestion. Different sugars have different metabolic paths.

items.q: Is sea salt or pink Himalayan salt healthier than ordinary table salt?
items.correct: No - they are all mostly sodium chloride and have the same effect on blood pressure; the 6 g daily limit applies to all of them.
items.misconception: Yes - 'natural' salts are healthy and don't count towards salt intake.
items.why: Marketing gives gourmet salts a health halo. Chemically they are around 97-99% sodium chloride, just like table salt; the trace minerals are nutritionally negligible. Sodium is what raises blood pressure - whatever crystal it arrives in.

items.q: Gram for gram, does sugar provide more energy than fat?
items.correct: No - fat provides 9 kcal per gram, while sugar (a carbohydrate) provides about 4 kcal per gram.
items.misconception: Yes - sugar is the most energy-dense nutrient.
items.random_wrong: Sugar and fat both provide zero energy.
items.why: Sugar's bad press makes students crown it most calorific. Fat has more than double the energy density of sugar - the key fact behind why fried foods and pastry are so energy-dense, and why the energy values (9 vs 4 kcal/g) are exam favourites.
The answer examiners want is: Fat. “Carbohydrate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 30 g. “10 g” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 4 kcal. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
